dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 6 responding for a call of suspected stroke with altered mental status at Apartment F.
Hi, I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
He was in the bathroom; he looked like he was having a stroke.
Did he lose consciousness when you found him or was he awake?
No, he was awake but confused and couldn't move when we found him behind the bathroom door.
Can you tell me your name, please?
... ... ... My name is Thomas ...
What day and date is it today, Mr. Thomas?
I ... I don't know. Maybe March 29.
Where are we now, Mr. Thomas?
I think my apartment. In Richmond.
Eyes are opening to voice. Pupils appear 1 mm. Unreactive on initial exam.
Can't move left side. Pupils are pinpoint.
Mr. Thomas, do you have any pain or pressure right now?
Yes. On my chest a bit. But I can't move my left side ...
Can you point with your left hand to the object on the bed beside you?
... I can't ... my left hand won't move.
We're going to assess your airway now. Can I take a quick look?
No stridor. Airway is patent. No signs of obstruction. Neck veins are flat.
Respirations are 10 breaths per minute, shallow. Equal chest rise, breathing sounds bilateral and normal.
We'll just check the saturation now. A mask will go over your nose and mouth just a moment, okay?
Our pulse is 101. Blood pressure is 179 over 91. Skin is warm and diaphoretic.
We're starting with some oxygen at low flow to support breathing while we get more measurements.
When did the symptoms first start for you, Mr. Thomas?
It started just after he got in the shower. He couldn't move and we had to help him out.
How has your health been lately? Do you have any chronic conditions?
He has high blood pressure and diabetes. Always has the bottle of pain pills.
What medications are you currently taking, Mr. Thomas?
I take something for pain. Aspirin, maybe.
Any allergies to medications?
No, none that I know of.
When was the last time you ate or drank before this?
He had some coffee this morning, but nothing else since yesterday.
Can you describe the pressure in your chest? Use a scale from 0 to 10 with 10 being the worst.
It's a pressure. Maybe a 5. And it feels heavy, steady.
Does it radiate to anywhere, like your arms, jaw, or back?
No. Just on my chest. But my left side feels ...
When did the pressure first start?
He's been like this about 20 minutes.
Skin is dry, no rashes, no bruising or scars noted.
Eyes are 1 mm, non-reactive. No head trauma. JVD absent.
Neck is stable. No stiffness or masses. Eyes are not equal or reacting in early stages.
Giving you a chest exam. Do you feel anything strange when I press here?
No pain with movement. But it's hard to feel anything on this side.
Sounds are clear and equal on both sides. Heart rhythm is regular. No murmurs or extra sounds.
Pressuring gently on your stomach-no pain or rigidity.
Left arm is not moving independently. No pain in the back or torso. No visual injuries on the legs.
I can't move my left arm at all ...
Pulse is currently 101.
Respiratory rate is 10.
Blood pressure is 179 over 91.
Glucose is unreadable.
SpO2 is 77 with supplemental oxygen applied.
EKG: Sinus rhythm, no acute infarct signs on the 4-lead monitor.
We will place you on a four-lead monitor and start some support lines to assist while we gather more details.
IV started on his right arm, 20-gauge cannula.
Giving normal saline 200 MLs via the IV.
Doing a 12-lead EKG as part of the protocol.

EKG: Abnormal. Right bundle branch block. Sinus rhythm with some blocks. Also shows left atrial enlargement, left anterior fascicular block, and some supraventricular premature complexes. No acute anterior infarct detected, but some old changes noted in V1/V2 and V6.
Based on critical trends in your vitals and symptoms, we're following the opioid overdose protocol.
Pulse now at 86. Respiratory rate is 14. Spo2 is 99. Blood pressure is 206 over 94. Blood sugar is 319.
Repeating EKG-still right bundle block and sinus rhythm. Capnography started for better airway monitoring.
Administering 0.5 mg of naloxone through the IV now.

Pulse returned to 78; respiratory rate is now 18. Blood pressure is 180 over 92. Spo2 is 100. Glasgow Coma Scale is 15 now. BFAST scale negative after naloxone administered. You responded consistently now.
How is the pressure now? Still 5 or has it changed?
It's better. Maybe a 3?
Mr. Thomas, when was your last known well time?
The last time he was fully okay was about 20 minutes ago.
What do you use to manage your health when you feel unwell, Mr. Thomas?
I take Fiorinal for the pain. Sometimes for headaches, sometimes for back issues.
Did you take any medications or substances other than the pain med recently?
I took a pinch. Just for old times. I've been clean since 2011, though. I had a stent placed about five years ago for a blockage.
Are you currently allergic to any medications or have other prior allergies?
No known drug allergies.
Skin and mental status are unchanged. Pupils are now 3 mm and equal.
Glasgow Coma Scale is 15 after naloxone. Patient is fully alert now.

We'll lift you up carefully. This might be a bit slow because we have to go down stairs. We will use the Reeves stretcher to help with the stairs! We'll have four of us carrying you down carefully and in a controlled way.
We'll get you into a stretcher and safely move you out from the stairs. We'll position you in a semi-Fowler's position in the ambulance for transport - elevated a bit for comfort and easy breathing.
You'll be heading to VCU Health Systems, the most appropriate facility for your condition.

Hospital was alerted and is prepared for our arrival. They were briefed on the medications, vital signs, and patient status. We're following the standard transport protocol for suspected opioid overdose. IV and O2 support will remain on board during transport.
Hospital nurse has been called. Information will be passed when we arrive. We're heading to VCU Health Systems.
